Armor.title: Armor
Armor.content:
Each type of armor has an associated armor die that absorbs damage as if it were hit points. It’s difficult to know in advance whether your opponents find a weakness in your armor. The armor die is therefore only rolled the first time you are hit in combat: the value of the die indicates the total number of HP your armor will absorb during that combat. You will roll the die again during the next combat scene, having had a little time in the meantime to rest and readjust your armor. As with all wear dice, if the result is 1-3, the armor is damaged, and the wear dice drops by one level. You will need to repair the armor to bring it back to its maximum.
The GM may impose a disadvantage when you perform certain actions while wearing armor (such as swimming, for example). If you are wearing armor that has a Resource Die higher than your Hit Die, you have a disadvantage on all physical and magic rolls (including in combat).
Putting on and adjusting armor requires a number of turns equal to the armor’s defensive value. Divide this by two if the character is assisted.

Chaotic-Magic.title: Chaotic Magic
Chaotic-Magic.content:
The effects of Chaotic Magic are strongly felt within The Dungeon. Indeed, The Dungeon is a thousand year old place where the forces of Chaos have dwelled. The Chaos Risk Die represents the degree of instability of magic in The Dungeon. It normally starts at a Δ12. If a character fails a Magic Roll, but still wants something to happen, they may roll the Chaos Die and let the magic happen! But be prepared for anything…
If Chaos falls below a Δ4, magicians can no longer call upon it. By default, it should upgrade by about one notch each mission.

























































RollResult1Error: The spells effect is reversed or dramatically transformed!2Mutation: The magic works, but transforms the character and leaves dramatic after-effects: deformity, scarring, etc.3Forgetfulness: The Spell works, but the character completely forgets about the spell, and will only remember it after a good nights sleep.4Drain Effect: The character loses a number of points equal to the cost ofthe spell in a randomly determined characteristic. These points are recovered at a rate of one per day.5Fireworks: Noise, light, no tangible effect, except that the magician’s clothes suddenly catch fire.6Power Spike: No magic takes effect, but the character regainsthe hit points spent on the spell.7Weakened Spell: The area of effect, the number of targets, the damage, everything is halved.8Lack of Control: Your magic has a negative side effect.9Power Leak: The spell works but costs twice its cost in HP (in total). If the character falls to 0 HP, they lose consciousness.10Delayed Effect: The magic takes effect normally… but in d4 turns.11Poor Aim: The spell affects a target other than the one you are aiming at, at the GM’s discretion.12BAM!!!: The effects, number of targets, or size of the spell are doubled.

Character-Creation.title: Character Creation
Character-Creation.content:
Roll 3d6 in order for Strength, Intelligence, Wisdom, Dexterity, Constitution, and Charisma. Swap two scores if you like.
Come up with three Traits. One of them must be your personality, expressed as an adjective.
Record your Hit Die (d6) and roll your hit points.
Choose two from the list below (you can take the same option twice): (a) Add d6 to a stat that is 10 or less (b) add another trait to your character (c) Martial Training (d) Specialist Training (e) Magical Training - Write Down Two Spells (g) Gain a Hit Die.
If you increased your Hit Die through Martial Training or chose to gain one or two more Hit Die, you can reroll your hit points with the corresponding die or die pool, and record your new hit point total (add 1 to your current HP if you don’t roll better than your initial hit points). Even if you didn’t increase your hit die, you can permanently spend one point of Constitution to reroll your hit point dice (Adding 1 to your previous result if you don’t roll better).
You always know the language of your Species and Common (and potentially languages related to your profession, e.g. Thieves Cant if you are a thief). Perform three Stat Checks against Intelligence, Wisdom, and Charisma. For each success, choose an additional language. Players may consult with one another to have a variety of languages from Residents of The Dungeon (orcs, trolls, troglodytes, etc) and Client Races (Elves, Dwarves, etc).
Choose two sections of the company that owe you a Δ4 favor and one that owes you a Δ6 favor.
As a Receptionist, you do not own much (equipment is provided for jobs by your employer).  But you possess 1 item. Roll on the Personal Effects table. This is one of your possessions, distinguished on Company Provided Equipment placed in your Kitbag.

Remember: In Dungeon Inc. you don’t dream up the perfect hero or work out the best combination of feats, abilities, and perks. You take what Fate gives you, and play as a character who has a long way to go in order to become Employee of the Month… if they survive that long. However, if you roll up a character and you really don’t think you’ll have fun playing this character, you’re welcome to completely restart (but keep the time of other players in mind).

Combat.title: Combat
Combat.content:
The rules of Combat in Dungeon Inc. are designed to depict exciting action scenes, rather than offering a tactical mini-game. This does not mean players will not have interesting decisions to make in combat, but rather that their choices are reflected in Narrative rather than Mechanical terms.
The golden rule of Dungeon Inc. combat is that each turn, to determine what happens players roll a single d20. The result is all or nothing. If a roll is successful, the player accomplishes what they were attempting. If they fail the roll, the player fails, and whatever consequences they were trying to avoid go through. NPCs therefore rarely make rolls to attack, as the results of their attacks are normally dictated by the results of Player Rolls.
Because of this system, the Player should always make sure they clearly know the consequences of failure before making a roll. At the same time, the GM should clearly describe what opponents are about to do at the beginning of a combat round.
Doug the #Agile #Goblin is cornered by three #Brooding #Owlbears. He may make an attack roll (A Strength Skill Check) to try and stab one of the owlbears with his dagger. If he succeeds, he rolls for damage to one of the owlbears, while weaving and dodging the clawing attacks of the other three. If he fails, he does not deal any damage to the owlbears, and must roll to take damage as he is clawed by the attacks of all three owlbears. Alternatively, Doug could have made an Agility Skill Check to try and maneuver himself away from the three owlbears. A Success would have gotten him out of the corner, whereas a Failure would mean he was pinned down by one of the owlbears, while being mauled by all attacks.

Disadvantage in combat is common. In order to reflect martial prowess, characters are given a disadvantage when facing an opponent who has a higher number of hit points. This disadvantage is compensated slightly by the fact that players get to decide who attacks whom via their actions choices.
To avoid Disadvantage, several characters can gang up on another. In hand-to-hand combat all the hit points on one side are added up, and the characters roll normally if they have a higher hit dice than their enemy. This only applies in a chaotic, close-quarters fight, like a medieval melee, where all involved are beating each other up in localized joyous chaos. If one person is fighting someone on one end of the room, and the other person is fighting someone else at the other end, then this is two separate melees, and the calculation is done separately for each fight.
The principle rule of the initiative system here is Common-Sense. An armed crossbow fires at a charging enemy before he can strike, a character who drinks a potion during combat will be attacked before the potion has time to take effect, the player who thinks of an action first goes first. The exact order is not so important; what matters is that the sequence is coherent within the fiction, and that everyone has a chance to enjoy themselves. The golden rule (based on the Macchiato Monsters system) is that each turn, players roll a single d20. It’s all or nothing (with the exception for when a PC has multiple attacks).
In combat, by maneuvering in interesting ways (which may require an ability roll) you can obtain advantage on some attack rolls.
Player: I roll between the paladin's legs to get behind him and strike him from behind.
GM: Perfect, make a DEX roll.
Player: Success!
GM: Excellent. The Paladin swings his weapon, narrowly missing you as you roll between his legs. For your next attack, you will roll 2d20 and keep the lower result.

Combat Made Easy in 6 Steps
The fact that the GM doesn’t systematically roll dice and that the players, most of the time, only roll one die per turn can be confusing for players and GMs used to more tactical systems. Here’s how the author of Macchiato Monsters describes his method as soon as blood is about to be shed:


Start of the turn. The GM describes the locations, threats, adversaries, and what they are about to do, especially where they are, where they are going, and who they are targeting.
It is essential that the overall situation is clear to everyone.


The first character has the opportunity to act – the GM asks the player what they want their character to do.


Who acts first depends on the situation? What needs to be resolved immediately before other aspects of the situation are changed? Who is in the lead? Who has their crossbow loaded? Who fell into the carnivorous eel pool?
Before any roll is made, the character’s situation is explained so that any potential risk and its consequences are clear.
This character’s turn is resolved by a die roll. Damage is assigned, and consequences are described.

The next character acts.

The sequence must make sense within the story. If there is no more logical sequence of actions, the GM goes around the table in the order the players are seated.
The GM describes the character’s situation, especially if the previous character’s action has changed the circumstances. There may be no one left to shoot at, or a spell gone awry may pose a new threat.


Repeat step 3 until all player characters have acted.


Have all the opponents done anything? If not, describe what they are doing and give those targeted a chance to react—this may require an out-of-sequence roll, for example, to avoid an attack or a spell.


End of turn. The GM refreshes the
description of the circumstances and
initiates the next turn

Employee-of-the-Month.title: Employee of the Month
Employee-of-the-Month.content:
The Benefits of being Employee of the Month are numerous. The host has access to the Management Offices for a few days. They eat with their superiors, have access to luxury shops and restaurants (if they can afford them), and become friendly with a director (of their choice), increasing the favor of all departments below that director. The Host’s suspicion level also resets to zero, and this is the only way to reduce Suspicion (other than receiving a warning).
If players are returning from a mission that their supervisor deems is a success, they get to make an Employee of the Month roll. Each player rolls a d20, and applies bonuses and penalties based on their characters perceived performance during the game:

Team Leaders Receive +1 since they are responsible for the success of the mission.
Exceptional Performance: A Supervisor may give individual hosts +1 if they consider they have gone above and beyond what was expected of them for the benefit of the company.
For each piece of damage or lost equipment, a -1 penalty is applied to the roll. This penalty does not apply to consumables.
Team Leaders may give a single demerit to one of their colleagues whom they consider to have not worked as part of a team, giving a -1 penalty.
For each point of Suspicion, take a -1 Penalty.

The current Employee of the Month competes with the players for the title. They Roll 1d20 + Δ6. If the players beat the Employee of the Month, their die upgrades.
If players receive less than zero on their Employee of the Month Roll, they receive a Warning. They are summoned by the Director of Living Resources, who makes it clear that this must not happen again. After three Warnings, a Receptionist is fired, and transferred to the Termination department. In practice, this takes two forms. First, one of the colleagues of the Receptionist is taken aside and offered a substantial bonus to kill their friend, in a way that looks like an accident. If colleagues refuse, Termination handles matters themselves, usually during a mission.

Equipment-Table.title: Equipment Table
Equipment-Table.content:
RollMelee Weapon + DamageRanged Weapon + Damage + UsageArmor2Truncheon d4Bag of Polished Stones d4 Δ10Leather Gloves Δ4+3Polearm# d6*A Belt of Throwing Daggers d4 Δ6Rusty Helmet Δ4+4Dagger d4Slingshot and Bag of Marbles d4 Δ10Leather Harness Δ65Cutlass d4Pouch of Darts d4 Δ6Leather Coat Δ4 and Round Shield6Shortsword d6Short Bow and Quiver of Arrows d6 Δ8Wooden Shield7Club + Nails d6One Bola d4 (Rock on rope)Boiled Leather Armor Δ68Whip d4Dueling Pistol with Lead amd Gunpowder d6§ Δ6Rusty Breastplate Δ69Lance d6/d6*One Javelin d6Leather Tunic and Toque Δ4+10Rapier d6A Sheath of Shurikens d4 Δ6Holey Chainmail Δ611Club d4Hand Crossbow with Bolts d6 Δ6Studded Leather Δ612Hatchet d6Composite Bow with Quiver of Arrows d6 Δ8Breastplate Δ613Gisarme d8*A Belt of Throwing Axes d6 Δ6Leather Gloves Δ4+ and a Small Shield14Scythe d6Culverin (Hand Cannon) with Lead and Gunpowder d8§ Δ6Poorly Fitted Lorica Hamata (Roman Mail Armor) Δ615MacheteA Case of Heavy ShurikensChainmail Δ816Flail d8/d8*Blunderbuss with Powder and Shot d6§ Δ8Chainmail Δ817Bardiche d6*Bladed Boomerang d8Rusty Hauberk Δ618Glaive d6Hunting Bow with Quiver d6 Δ8Breastplate Δ619Schimitar d8Repeating Crossbow d8 Δ8Moldy Leather and Rusty Chainmail Δ620Spade d8*Fustibale (Really Long Slingshot) with Bag of Marbles d6 Δ8Rusty Plate Δ821Wakizashi d6A Francisque (Medieval Throwing Axe) d8Leather and Mail Δ6 with Shield22Sword and Parrying Dagger d8*A Frame (Germanic Javelin) d8Ring Armor Δ623Katana d8Slingshot with Steel Balls d6 Δ8Gambeson Δ6



Two-handed melee weapons have a damage advantage. Weapons marked dX/dX* can be wielded with one or two hands.




can be combined with one and only one armor already worn (+1 notch to the use die of said armor).


§ These gunpowder weapons can wound 1d4 grouped targets at the GM’s discretion. Gunpower damage is doubled, but unreliable, a roll of d3 on the usage die causing the weapon to explode, inflicting it’s damage on the shooter as well as the attacker. It takes a few minutes to restore the weapon to it’s original Δ.

Favors.title: Favors
Favors.content:
Favors are a form of social debt with Dungeon Inc. Because you once helped a colleague, covered for them when they made a mistake, or because you go for pints with them at the Company Bar, they may help you in return. Characters can increase the value of their favors, or obtain favors from new departments, by becoming Employee of the Month, doing favors for colleagues, and carrying out temporary assignments for departments other than the Receptionists. Favors can be used in two ways, Helping Hands and Services.
A Helping Hand gives you a slight advantage in a certain situation. The player describes a short flashback related to the current action happening. For example, “I went Duck Hunting with Sir Smythington of the Living Relations Department, and I remember his advice on handling muskets”, which gives advantage to a Dexterity Stat Check when making a ranged attack. The player must describe their relation by coming up with three traits for the character, and the GM notes them down so that they can become a recurring character. The player then rolls the associated Favor Die to see if the Favor downgrades.
A Service is used to gain information, benefit from a direct advantage from someone, or take narrative control. For example, “I ask Jakaline, the Werewolf in Living Resources, which of my colleagues reported me to her boss”. The GM decides to give the information. Or the Player can take narrative control: “Our cartographer told me there was a trapdoor in this corridor that hasn’t been marked on the map. So the client falls into it, right?“. When using a Service, the associated Favor Die always decreases, without a roll. A Service Die can also be used to gain Advantage on a Damage Roll of an attack, both when dealing damage and taking damage.
Player: Ouch! I Missed my attack roll.
GM: The Dragon breathes on you, roll a d12 for damage.
Player: Wait! We did an avoidance training course with Grolzub, the #haughty #filthy goblin from Living Resources in charge of training. He taught us how to roll to avoid projectiles. I'm going to use this technique to get an advantage on the damage die.
GM: Okay you have advantage on your Damage Die.

In general, GMs should not be too demanding regarding the use of Helping Hands, whereas Services should only be granted if plausible, and which are not completely disruptive to the game. It is also necessary to weigh the risk taken by the colleague to whom the character is asking for the service in order to decide whether they accept.

Injuries-and-Death.title: Injuries and Death
Injuries-and-Death.content:
At 0HP, a character has taken a mortal wound. If they fail a CON check, they are dead, and their name will be engraved on The Dungeon’s memorial wall. If they pass, they recover, with 1HP, and a terrible injury, giving them an associated negative trait.
Marna the #mystic #gnome falls into a trapdoor, and the damage reduces her to 0 HP. She makes a CON play by using a favor to gain an advantage (a colleague from Termination taught her to minimize the impact of falls). 12 and 7, it's a success site. Marna regains consciousness, but her leg is broken and will never return to normal. Marna adds a #wobbly-foot trait to her character. Once healed, she will be at a disadvantage on her running rolls and other rolls requiring good leg coordination.

Magical-Training.title: Magical Training
Magical-Training.content: Spells are loosely defined in a short phrase, such as “Control the Undead”, “Sense Magic”, “Battle Fire”, “Call Animals From The Depths”. You must decide which spells your character knows. The more precise the wording (and therefore the narrower the application of the spell), the easier it is to use. You start the game with a focus of Δ6.

Magical-Weapons.title: Magical Weapons
Magical-Weapons.content: At Dungeon Inc. magical items are rather rare. The meager salary of a Receptionist makes it very difficult to afford such luxurious items, and management frowns on the use, since they prefer to know exactly what their employees are capable of. If a character possesses a magic weapon, they have stolen it off a Client. Because of this, each time a Receptionist uses a magical weapon with a visible effect, they generate a suspicion point.

Martial-Training.title: Martial Training
Martial-Training.content:
Each time you choose the Martial Training option, upgrade your Hit Die by one notch (from d6 to d8, to a maximum of d10). You hit Die represents both the damage ceiling of the weapons your character uses, and the maximum armor dice you may have without taking disadvantage on physical related rolls.
As an example, with a d8 Hit Die, a d4 dagger inflicts d4 damage, and a d10 great sword inflicts d8 damage. You can wear Δ10 plate armor, but this imposes a disadvantage on almost all physical rolls. On the other hand, wearing Δ8 chainmail incurs no such disadvantage.

Mission-Structure.title: Mission Structure
Mission-Structure.content:
The Player’s Supervisor meets with the players, giving them the Context of the Mission, a description of the Customer Area, and the Objectives of the Mission. Team Leaders are assigned. They are responsible for the smooth managing of the mission, have (theoretically) authority over the rest of the team, and also are blamed if the mission goes wrong.
The characters head to the Cartography Library, an immense room filled with labelled filing cabinets. Cartographer hands over the map of the Area to the Mission Leader. Cartographers frown upon receptionists who take it upon themselves to annotate the map. The Design Department, like other creatives, have certain obsessions. Knowing which creator created a given dungeon level can help anticipate the design of the dungeon.
The Characters travel to the Equipment Department. It is a mixture between a large bazaar and a theatre costume room. The Bursar distributes the Kitbags to the players. Each player rolls a d20 + Seniority on the package table. Usually, Kitbags are hastily put together, and it is rare for them to be designed for a specific mission. Favors with the Equipment Department can be used to gain advantage on this role. Similarly, a player’s relation with the Quartermaster can give advantage or disadvantage. The player receives equipment based on their roll, in addition to Δ4 torches and a healing potion (Δ6 up to the fifth rank of seniority and Δ8 from the sixth rank onwards). All loaned equipment must be returned in the same condition. While the Equipment Department is lenient about consumable usage, a lost or broken weapon, or damaged armor will be held against the Receptionist and may negatively impact Employee of the Month awards.
The Receptionists take the Elevator down to the appropriate level. A platform hoisted by ropes. The elevator is the perfect place to meet colleagues, get teased, and hear gossip. Sometimes a Bard is on duty in the elevator to help pass the time. The Elevator is the best protected spot in The Dungeon, as it is essential that Adventurers never come across it. The Corridors connecting Client Areas to The Elevator are protected by a complex series of Secret Passages to limit the risk of discovery. The last Secret Passage is always equipped with a Peephole to ensure there are no clients of residents on the other side.
The Players accomplish, or fail to accomplish, the assigned mission. They then report to their Supervisor again for a debriefing.

Personal-Effects.title: Personal Effects
Personal-Effects.content:
A Wedding Ring.
A Tooth from Leogradanicus, the Crimson Dragon from Floor B12.
A Magnificent and Strange Musical Instrument Made by an Artisan of Yesteryear.
A Helmet which Amplifies your Hearing.
A Truth Potion Obtained (or stolen from) a Colleague in the Termination Department.
A Scroll said to Contain a Spell to Banish Demons.
A Compass which does not always point north.
A vial containing a liquid that emits a powerful light when shaken.
A powder that makes any dish edible (and delicious). Δ6 uses.
A Snake Whistle (Still Sought by Snake Men).
Mithril Lock-Picking Tools bearing the initials A.L.
A Roughly Crafted Iron Crown.
A Strange Ornamental Dagger with Sinuous Blade and Handle Decorated By Snakes.
A Stone Eyeball from an Ancient Primitive Idol.
A Parchment Acknowledging a Debt to One of the Bigwigs at Dungeon Inc.
The Lucky Paw of the Law Rabbit in the Dungeon. If only you had the other three!
A mirror that even vampires can see their reflection in.
A mummified hand that snaps its fingers when placed on a flat surface.
A set of knucklebones said to be from the left foot of the Lich Aacarcerax.
A set of uncleanable bloodstained keys.

Personal-Objectives.title: Personal Objectives
Personal-Objectives.content:
A personal objective is a narrative goal set by the players, with the goal of bringing Life to Dungeon Inc. outside of it’s pure mission structure. The goals should focus on objectives achieved by interacting with the organization rather than those achieved purely through dungeon exploration (although feel to break free from this structure when it would be fun, e.g. “Get Revenge on the Resident Troll on Floor B37”). Personal Goals are needed to obtain higher levels of Seniority.
Personal Goals should neither be too easy to achieve (e.g. “Talk to X”) or too long term (“Become the Best Wizard in the Company”). Such an objective might be broken down into a sequence of several personal objectives. Simple Personal Objectives include “Find out why Floor B13 is closed to customers and employees”, “Convince the Head of Reception to introduce a Suggestion Box”, “Organize a Candlelit Dinner with the Employee of the Month”. A long term goal like “Get Rid of the Undead Employee of the Month by Resurrecting Him” might be broken down into 1. “Find a Book of Necromancy”, 2. “Find Someone who can Understand the Book”, and 3. Convince that person to bring the Employee of the Month back to life. Each is an individual personal objective.

Primary-Colleagues.title: Primary Colleagues
Primary-Colleagues.content:
Before playing, the players collectively create profiles for four of the main Dungeon Inc. employees whom they will interact with during their job. Each employee is described by the players using traits, four traits per colleague (go around the room each adding a trait). Then the players decide on a name and gender (if relevant) for the character.

Supervisor: Works in the Reception Department, and is the Boss of the Characters. This character is in charge of assigning missions, evaluating them, and supporting (or preventing) their application for Employee of the Month.
Bursar: Works in the Supply Department, and is in charge of preparing the host’s its for each mission, handing them over, and check their equipment’s condition upon return.
The Cartographer: Works in the Surveying Department, and is charge of giving the hosts maps of the areas where their missions will take them.
Employee of the Month: Works in Reception. Your rival. They may not always be employee of the Month, but they are the ones they aspire to imitate and replace.

Once these characters are created, each player determines a positive relationship with one profile, and a negative relationship with another, briefly describing the nature of that relationship.






























ColleaguePositive RelationshipNegative RelationshipSupervisorThe Supervisor may not favor you openly, but they like you, and that influences their judgement when defending your case before the Employee of the Month Committee. Mechanically, this gives you an Advantage on all Employee of the Month Rolls.The Supervisor does not like you, and will only defend your case before the Employee of the Month Committee if given no choice. Take a Disadvantage on all Employee of the Month Rolls.BursarA positive relationship with the Bursar gives Advantage on all Pack Rolls.Your packs are generally poorly packed. You suffer Disadvantage on all Pack Rolls.CartographyYou benefit from the Cartographer’s Good Advice. Once per Game, the host can benefit from a Surveying Service.The Cartographer gives you bad advice (or you didn’t listen). In a room of the GM’s choice (presented on the map), the character suffers disadvantage on all rolls.Employee of the MonthThis is excellent for your reputation in the Department. Each time you are promoted, increase a favor in a department by one level.The Employee of the Month gossips behind your back. Each time you are promoted, you receive one point of Suspicion.

Resource-Die.title: Resource Die
Resource-Die.content: Some items (food, ammunition, favors, armor quality) deteriorate over time. We use resource die to represent that deterioration, indicated with a Δ. Each time the item is used, the resource die is rolled. A result of 1-3 means that the resource has been significantly depleted. The die is downgraded to a low result, from Δ12 to Δ10 to Δ8 to Δ6 to Δ4 to nothing. When a result of 1-3 is obtained on a Δ4, the resource is exhausted (no more food, no more arrows, no more favor with the Welcome Center, etc.).

Salary.title: Salary
Salary.content:
At the end of each mission, Receptionists receive a Salary according to their rank





















































RankSalary1Δ4 Copper2Δ6 Copper3Δ8 Copper4Δ10 Copper5Δ12 Copper6Δ4 Silver7Δ6 Silver8Δ8 Silver9Δ10 Silver10Δ12 SilverOccasionally (especially if on good terms with The Foreman), players may receive a bonus for an exceptionally good job done, and receive an additional salary.
Hosts can spend their wages at the Brawler’s Bazaar and the Silver Goblet.

Seniority.title: Seniority
Seniority.content:
There are no Levels in Dungeon Inc. only a Seniority Ranking. To increase your Seniority Rank by one, you must complete a number of successful missions (in which the objective set by your Foreman was achieved) equal to your seniority rank. In addition, starting from the 2nd rank, you must accomplish a personal objective that changes at each rank.
When leveling up, a character can gain two of the following options:

Add 1 to a Characteristic of your Choice.
Gain 1 Hit Die, taking that roll as your new hit points if higher than your original (or adding 1 to your previous if lower than or equal).
Learn a new spell (Magic Training Required)
Increase the Δ of a Specialized Training by one level. Can only be taken once per Specialized Training.

At Ranks 4, 7, and 10, you may also add a new training or a new trait.

Shields.title: Shields
Shields.content: If holding a shield, you have advantage on Dodge Rolls when being shot at. A player can also sacrifice their shield to negate all damage from an attack, if this makes narrative sense.

Specialist-Training.title: Specialist Training
Specialist-Training.content:
Specialized Training reflects a unique talent. Ideally, it should be associated with one of the character’s traits (often their species or profession). It works Δ4 times per day and does not require a roll of the dice, but must be activated by the player. Specialized training is the only way to gain advantage on combat rolls.
Discuss with the GM to establish what this power does, particularly if it confers a mechanical advantage or contravenes standard rules. Powerful Trainings may be offset by Disadvantages to be agreed upon. Unlike Spells, the mechanical effects of Specialized Trainings do not increase with seniority.
Some examples include:

A Pet (which alerts you to the presence of customers).
Combat Rage (Doubles the Damage of the next successful attack).
Fire Breather (d12 damage, but on a roll of 1-3, the character catches fire).
Silver Tongue (a crowd will listen to you, as long as you speak without paying attention to what is happening around you).
Dirty Tricks (automatic advantage on a combat roll, attack, or damage).
Regeneration (Regain all your HP).

Stat-Check.title: Stat Check
Stat-Check.content:
Whenever your character is in danger, or attempts something risky, you roll a Stat Check. Roll a d20. As long as it is less than or equal to the relevant stat, chosen by the referee, you pass. Rolling a 1 or a 20 is a critical roll, and doubles the effect of the roll, or brings an additional consequence into play.
Trait Rolls are only made if there is a risk of failure. A Djinn would not have to make a DEX Stat Check to pass over a trap door, but might have to make a CON Stat Check in a strong wind to avoid being blown away.

Suspicion.title: Suspicion
Suspicion.content: Hosts are not supposed to help themselves to Client’s Items and other Items left around The Dungeon. When they do so (regardless of whether they are seen or not), they accumulate Suspicion Points, which represent the degree of mistrust superiors have towards them. When their Suspicion is high, they risk a formal warning from their boss, or worse… The main mechanical use of the Suspicion stat is on Employee of the Month Rolls.

The-Dungeon.title: The Dungeon
The-Dungeon.content:
A huge tower sitting atop a mountain, with a league-wide base. From the foot of the mountain, it is impossible to see the top. There are many legends about the place. But they all have one commonality; an adventurer can make their fortune there, if they are willing to risk their life.
But why is The Dungeon filled with such disparate bands of monsters. Who resets the traps? Why, after being plundered so many times, is The Dungeon still filled with such riches.
The answer is an extremely well-kept secret. Within The Dungeon, there is a company, whose mission is to lure adventurers into the dungeon, eliminate them, and rob them. You can think of the operation like a casino - some adventurers escape with a meager supply of loot, but most do not, and so The House Always Wins. The wealth contributed by the adventurers who lose their lives there allows the organization to survive and (in good years) even to prosper.
Your characters are employees of Dungeon & Co. They are even the essential cog in the organization: receptionists. Their job is to welcome customers, i.e. adventurers, and to kill them and steal their belongings. Always remember: A Good Customer is a Dead Customer.
Below is a description of several key members of the customer, and a description of the heirarchy.
The Chief Administrator
The Chief administrator is a man called Mr. Noir. He is the founder of the company and sole owner of the corporation. Not much is known about him, and few claim to have ever met him (and those that have are all bigwigs). Mr. Noir, apart from his inner circle (personal assistants, secretaries, etc) has only two direct subordinates, each heads of one of the divisions of the company: The Strategy Division and the Operation Division. Since little is known about Mr. Noir, this fuels rumors: is he a demon hatching a long-term plan to corrupt humanity? Or perhaps a wealthy heir who needed something to do with his family fortune? Or is he a vampire who haunts the corridors after hours to feed at night? In short, since no one knows anything, anything is possible…
The Strategy Division
The Strategy Division is in charge of the long-term vision of the organization. This division includes the Treasury Department, Living Resources Department, and Marketing Department.
The Treasury Department
The Treasury Department manages everything related to the finances of Dungeon Inc., whether income and expenses (Accounting) or investments (Patron Relations).
Accounting
Accounting draws up the annual budget for Dungeon Inc. and determines resources allocated to each department, as well as securing the revenue of the company. This involves not only collecting all the booty collected by the Receptionists, but also reselling items of value. Accounting prefers to hire employees who are good with numbers, and unpretentious as possible - rigor and discipline are virtues. Therefore, there are a high proportion of creatures with superior intelligence (vampires, mind-flayers, etc) but also structured and rigid-minded creatures (Constructs, Golems, Modrons, etc).
Patron Relations
Responsible for drawing in sponsors likely to invest in the development of Dungeon Inc. as well as maintaining good relations with them (through events, cocktail parties, symposiums, etc). Also negotiates via accounting the renumeration of sponsors during annual reviews. Patron Relations primarily employs monsters who have a network of contacts in the upper echelons of society, or who are charismatic and sticklers for etiquette. Employees generally impress with their elegant appearance and ability to charm (naturally or magically). Therefore there are a higher proportion of elves, devils, incubi, succubi, lamias, etc.
Department of Live Resources
The Living Resources department is responsible for recruiting employees, paying salaries, and managing contracts, but also for relations with inhabitants of the dungeon.
Recruitment Department
The Recruitment department is responsible for identifying the skills required for the various missions of Dungeon Inc. and recruiting the right candidates. Candidates are drawn from creatures who frequent the dungeon, or in very rare cases, from Clients. However, sometimes the Recruitment Department may look outside the Dungeon for candidates with specific profiles. Recruitment generally hires employees who are sensitive to the needs of others, but also skilled negotiators. In addition, the team insists on diversity among its members in order
to maximize the chances of establishing a rapport with a candidate regardless of their background.
Internal Relations Department
The role of internal relations stems from a single observation: why send costly resources to eliminate Clients when you can put adversaries in their path who will do the job without getting paid? The role of internal relations is therefore to attract monsters to suitable levels of The Dungeon and ensure they stay in their place, and eliminate the adventurers (or at least weaken them before the arrival of a team of Receptionists). Internal relations are undeniably the team that enjoys the greatest diversity in terms of profiles, particularly in terms of races represented. Indeed, it is necessary to know how to play on the affinities of races with little developed intelligence to convince them to settle (or stay) in an area of the Dungeon despite the potential dangers. Its employees are often people who have traveled extensively and know the world well, but are also smooth talkers and unscrupulous.
Termination Department
Keeping the existence of Dungeon Inc. a secret is of vital importance to the corporation’s success. Discretion is key, and that is where Termination comes in. officially, Termination manages the termination of contracts with employees. Unofficially, they are in charge of eliminating anyone who might be likely to spill the beans, and former employees
are at the top of the list. They are also responsible for terminating the contracts of
indiscreet employees. The selection criteria for joining the Termination team are quite simple: you must be an excellent killer; you must be discreet; you must be loyal. Beyond that,
Termination has no use for any other qualities or flaws, so you’ll find all kinds of brutes who know how to control themselves, and silent assassins.
Marketing Department
The central role of Marketing is to attract customers to the Dungeon. To do this, it is necessary to design attractive areas and carry out appropriate promotional campaigns. To be effective, it is therefore necessary to know the customers well.
Design Department
in order to maintain a constant flow of adventurers, you need to attract them by offering them something new. Although the Dungeon is a place with an unpredictable geography, it remains a unique location on a vast map that has no shortage of other points of interest for adventurers. Whether they are greedy, reckless, or righteous, they must be confronted with situations (concepts, in Design jargon) that are new and intriguing. The Design team therefore invents areas, with appropriate histories and recommendations for Advertising. The Packaging team is then responsible for manufacturing them to the required specifications. The Design Department are a mix of creatives (whose role is to come up with concepts) and architects (whose role is to
turn their colleagues’ ideas into plans and, if necessary, temper them with a touch of pragmatism). The team is rounded out by managers, whose role is to evaluate the costs and expected revenues of each concept. The Design department is therefore made up of intelligent people who are generally free-spirited (at least more so than average).
Publicity Department
All of the Publicity Department’s intellectual and financial resources are devoted to getting to know customers (this is profiling) and classifying them as greedy (attracted by gold), reckless (attracted by the thrill of risk) and vigilant (attracted by the possibility of putting an end to evil)
(attracted by the thrill of risk) and avengers (attracted by the possibility of putting an end to evil) and luring them into the areas most likely to cause their demise. Advertising uses many
methods: spreading rumors in inns, scattering treasure maps, raids, and kidnappings. In short, anything that will motivate adventurers to come and try their luck in the Dungeon. The Department obviously seeks to attract creative profiles, but also people with a somewhat mercenary mentality, who are likely to think cynically about what is most likely to appeal to a particular type of adventurer. In addition to creative teams, La Réclame employs artists (to design the accessories needed for advertising campaigns) and field teams, who work primarily outdoors.
Operations Division
The Operations Division is responsible for ensuring the day-to-day running of Donjon & Co.: maintenance, supervision, expansion, and, of course, customer service.
Facilities Department
The Facilities Department undoubtedly has the widest variety of tasks in the organization: feeding employees, but also resetting traps, ensuring areas comply with regulations, and mapping them.
Department of Maintainance
Maintenance is the backbone of the Dungeon, ensuring both the core mission by making sure that areas open to customers are ready, and that the rest of the staff can work in good conditions. Most employees believe that Maintenance spends its time resetting traps and repairing damage done by customers (or vandals) to the areas. But Maintenance is also
responsible for the living and working quarters of Donjon & Co., the various canteens, and a thousand other services that employees are happy to enjoy on a daily basis. Maintenance hires all kinds of profiles, but is primarily looking for candidates who are hard workers. Maintenance activities are, for the most part, very physical, and the department therefore favors strong, willing employees with recognized mechanical skills. There are more dwarves and gnomes here than in any other department.
Supplies Department
The Supplies Department has a dual role. On the one hand, the department manages all the equipment needs of the company’s various activities: the packages for the hosts (obviously), but also the tools for Maintenance, the supplies for Surveying, the crystal balls for Supervision, etc. On the other hand, Equipment manages the collection of customers’ equipment and valuables. They must therefore identify what is valuable (and which is transferred to Entries and Exits) and what can be reused for Donjon & Cie missions. There are very different jobs within Equipment and therefore, inevitably, very varied profiles. The stewards who manage equipment needs are a combination of office clerks (endowments, inventories, storage, etc.), craftsmen (damaged equipment must be repaired, anything that can be useful for the company’s missions must be transformed, etc.) and managers. In general, In general, Equipment favors manual and logistical profiles.
Surveying Department
Surveying is in charge of both regularly visiting the various areas of the dungeon (those open to customers and others) and maintaining accurate maps of them. These maps are not only used by Receptionists when they are on assignment, but also by Marketing (to avoid having to reinvent the wheel with each new area), Maintenance (to know where work needs to be done), Supervision (to locate Customers), and so on. In short, it is a vital function of Dungeon Inc. and yet probably the most employees complain about. To work in surveying requires observation, love of walking, and a precise hand and attention to detail. Surveying relies on versatile individuals who go into the field and draw or update maps. It employs many gnomes, who possess both nimble hands and a taste for exploration. It also boasts the highest concentration of glasses and monocles: Surveying is not kind to the eyesight of its employees.
Reception Department
Reception is the backbone of Donjon & Co.’s business model, as its members are responsible for eliminating customers and robbing them. It is (by far) the largest team within the company, but also the one with the highest mortality rate.
Supervision Department
The core mission of Supervision is to know times where The Dungeon’s guests are so that Receptionists can be directed when necessary. It’s possible (but these are undoubtably malicious rumors) that these methods are used to spy on Dungeon Inc. employees. Supervision employees use a combination of mechanical means (primarily alarms) and magical ones (crystal balls, remote viewing spells, etc). Therefore, more than in any other department of the organization, magical creatures and other spellcasters have a place in Supervision. An affinity for magic is the main criterion, although having more eyes than normal or other heightened senses is also valued.
Reception Department
Reception is the backbone of Donjon & Co.’s business model, as its members are responsible for eliminating customers and robbing them. It is (by far) the largest team within the company, but also the one with the highest mortality rate. Reception is not very discerning about the nature of its employees, given that their lifespan is limited. That being said, an aptitude for violence or an ability to use offensive magic are obviously prized. So you’ll find all kinds of creatures who are used to facing adventurers, from minotaurs to vampires, ogres, trolls, and mummies. The only restrictions on employment within Reception are the ability to follow orders and the physical ability to move around the corridors of the Dungeon.
Expansion Department
The Dungeon is not a static place and, despite its immense size, must constantly expand to open up new areas and attract more and more customers. It is the role of Expansion Management to ensure this
Exploration Department
In order to attract more and more new customers, we must constantly offer them new areas to explore. The good news is that the depths of the Dungeon are virtually infinite. The bad news is that most of these places are dangerous and little known. The role of Exploration is to prospect and secure them so that we can build new customer areas. The role of Exploration is to prospect and secure them so that we can build new customer areas there. The qualities required to work in Exploration are both physical (you have to be hard-working, willing to take risks, in short, be in exceptional physical shape) and mental (you must be fearless and have an iron will). Certain species that are particularly well-suited to underground life are favored, whether they be troglodytes, who are flexible and comfortable in underground tunnels and underwater, or deep dwarves, who are experts in rock and comfortable in the abyss.
Reconditioning Department
The role of Packaging is to build customer areas in new parts of the Dungeon secured by Exploration. The concepts are produced by Design (within Marketing), but it is Reconditioning
that manages their manufacture. This often involves compromises between the two entities
to limit costs (but also because some concepts from Marketing are… a bit crazy?). Most employees in Packaging are manual laborers or site managers. Their profiles are not very different from those in Maintenance: they are strong, hard-working, mechanically skilled, and resourceful. While the job is difficult, it is also relatively low-risk, so Packaging employees are often loyal to their department and stay there for a long time. This is the department with the most old timers.

Traits.title: Traits
Traits.content:
Traits define your character: their origins, their occupation, their personality, and so on. A trait is a phrase, of one or more words, describing what your character is, does, belongs to, or comes from. One of your character’s three traits must express their personality, demeanor, or defining characteristic. One of the traits can be the species of your character (or if not, your character belongs to the boring yet reliable default of fantasy worlds: a human). For example, traits could be:

What Your Character Is: a Dwarf, a Green Ogre, a Flower Fairy. One of the pleasures of the game is a chance to play a monster, but its not mandatory. If a character wants to play a ranger, an elf, or a halfling, they can play such a character. It may be more interesting to have a mix of monstrous races and classic adventurer tropes in one group. Whatever species you choose to play, your characters must be intelligent enough that they can actually be employed in Dungeon Inc, and cannot be large enough so as to make traversing the corridors of The Dungeon impossible (in general, anything larger than an ogre is too large). There may be some ludo narrative dissonance if you choose to play a monster that is very powerful, but I will not limit such choices.
Their Occupation: A tracker, a sorcerer, a merchant, a travelling musician, etc.
A Group They Belong To that can provide help or resources (a magician’s club, a riddle enthusiasts society).
Where Your Character Comes From: Grew up in the City of Thieves, survived the Wilderness Wars, raised in The Dungeon, etc.
How They Relate To The World: Cautious, Inquisitive, Polite, Brutal.

We use # to denote Traits.
Examples:

A Cruel Wise Minotaur
A Jaded Elf Archer
A Militant Warrior Mage (and thus a human)
A Sickly Djinn Bard
An Ettin Fighter with Split-Personality
A Bronze-Golem Bodyguard Made-Of-Valuable-Material

The GM might ask questions about your traits to clarify what they mean.
When you make a roll and your trait could help you, you gain an advantage. A trait can also allow you to perform an action that another character could not perform (such as climbing a vertical wall or flying). On the other hand, if you make a roll and your trait could make your attempt more difficult, you gain a disadvantage (e.g. if you are a suspected member of a syndicate, and are negotiating with the Director of Living Resources, or you are an ogre trying to walk a plank over a pit).
GM: Make a DEX Roll to jump over the flow of lava.
PC: I'm an #Agile Goblin. Does that give me an advantage.
GM: Of Course!

Traits are rarely used in combat; killing people with a pointy stick is a dangerous think to do, and these rules aren’t going to make it easy for anyone. On the other hand, Specialist Training can give advantage on combat rolls.
Remember that traits never imply having any special powers. They only give advantage or disadvantage on rolls.
